summary: Gruber chronicles the first day of Apple's App Store, noting that download counts were initially visible on each app's detail page, effectively making sales figures public. He examines the ratio of free-to-paid downloads for apps like Twitterrific and Exposure, finding roughly 40-to-1 ratios favoring free versions. He also covers the store's reliability, the sandboxing model that cleanly removes all app data on deletion, and early usability issues with AOL's AIM client. The piece highlights a design tension in Apple's recommendation that developers put settings in the system-wide Settings app, which most developers ignored, creating user confusion.
key_points: Download counts on the App Store effectively made sales figures public on day one, with Sega's Super Monkey Ball earning over $109K in under a day, though Apple later reset the counts to zero
Free-to-paid download ratios for Twitterrific and Exposure were remarkably similar at roughly 42-to-1 and 48-to-1, revealing early consumer resistance to paying for apps with free alternatives
The App Store's sandboxing model ensures clean deletion — removing an app removes all its data, preferences, and files with nothing left behind
Apple's recommendation to use the system-wide Settings app for preferences created confusion when most developers ignored it, making the compliant apps seem settings-less
The store was reliable on day one but the real test would come with the iPhone 3G launch and 2.0 OS update the following day
money_quotes: Given that the only way to download a non-free app is to buy it, it more or less puts sales figures out in the open.
That's $109,440 in revenue in under a day — about $76K for Sega, and $33K for Apple.
Second thoughts regarding the open kimonos?
Delete an app from the home screen and there's no sign of it left behind.
Foolproof, almost — a very friendly conceptual design for typical users.

If most third-party apps display their settings screens themselves, then when users do encounter an app that uses the system-wide Settings app, they're very likely to assume that the app simply doesn't have any settings.
key_insight: The App Store's first day revealed fundamental tensions — between transparency and control in sales data, between free and paid app economics, and between Apple's design guidelines and developer practice — that would define the platform for years to come.
